benchmark: Strategic Asset Allocation v1.2 (Growth, target CPI + 5%)
sources: NexSky's own view, informed by UBS, Syz, Pictet, J.P. Morgan and Luccai plus family-office peer data. Not a consensus average.
stance: Constructive but more selective: the late-June AI sell-off began to validate our broadening call. Risk-on equities tilted to quality, defensives and non-US; short duration with a belly hedge; gold and a long dollar as hedges; modestly more constructive on energy.

scorecard.view: Late-June saw a narrow mega-cap and semis sell-off (Nasdaq -4.2%, semis -7.7%, Mag7 -5.9%) while the equal-weight S&P and Russell 2000 rose: our broaden-do-not-add-to-mega-cap call. Below UBS (Attractive). 6-month softened on valuation-reset risk.

Outperformed on a defensive composition (STOXX -0.9% vs Nasdaq -4.2%) and ECB survey inflation expectations eased to 3.5%, supportive for domestic names. Wide valuation gap. More constructive than UBS (Neutral). Conviction raised.
TOPIX -3.6% in the tech sell-off, but BoJ normalisation, record earnings and governance are intact. Overweight held; near-term softened on semis beta. Aligned with UBS and Syz. Watch the yen squeeze.

Hit hardest in the drawdown (MSCI EM -4.6%, KOSPI -6%, TAIEX -5.1%) on semis concentration and a strong dollar. Structural case intact, near-term softened. Take EM in equity form, underweight EM local and FX (the Pictet barbell).

ECB survey inflation expectations easing to 3.5% and a growth-scare bid soften the near-term bearish call, but heavy supply and the underweight-duration tilt stay. With Syz and Pictet. Prefer the short end; add belly as an air-pocket hedge.

Fed held 3.50-3.75% but the dot plot flipped to a 2026 hike and the year-end PCE projection rose to 3.6%. The 5-year offers the best risk-reward; belly for carry and as an air-pocket hedge, reduce the long end. Against UBS (Attractive).
Fiscal tail under Burnham, long gilts above 5%, a wider gilt-Bund spread. Avoid; the risk premium is rising. Unchanged.
Decent carry but spreads tight, around 80bps, with little cushion into risk-off. Carry only.
Very tight spreads, around 300bps, late in the cycle, into a rising risk-off and AI-air-pocket probability. Not paid for the risk. Conviction raised.
A strong dollar pressures local and FX, while hard-currency carry is acceptable. The Pictet barbell.

Down about 25% from the January peak to around 4,190 dollars, the near-term real-yield and dollar headwind we flagged. Structural demand intact (central banks bought 244t in Q1, J.P. Morgan sees about 755t for 2026; year-end house targets 4,900 to 6,000 dollars). A double hedge for the air-pocket and stagflation tails. Use the pullback to top up toward 5%.

Turning modestly more constructive. Renewed Middle East ceasefire uncertainty and the war in Iran put energy back in the Fed's inflation reasoning and re-priced the geopolitical optionality higher. Energy equity as a tail hedge.
Copper is structural on AI, power and electrification. We like copper, with UBS.

The Fed dot plot flipping to a hike against an ECB whose inflation expectations are easing widens the rate differential in the dollar's favour. DXY firm. Against UBS's EUR/USD 1.20 bullish-euro call. Conviction raised.
The SNB at 0% caps appreciation, with a haven bid on Hormuz risk. A three-way split, kept small and flagged.
The sharpest growth-versus-value divergence of the year and high single-stock dispersion reward macro, multi-strategy and low-net long-short. Conviction raised.
The core engine, deployed through commitment pacing. A structural overweight, with the SAA at 30% privates.
Logistics, data centres, residential, real-estate debt and energy-transition infrastructure. Selective.
Weak momentum and a risk-off backdrop. Reduce beyond the strategic 2%.

peerComparison.note: OW overweight, N neutral, UW underweight, n/a not clearly published. Translated from each house's most recent published view; as-of date shown under each name. Some views are nuanced (UBS prefers short-duration bonds; BlackRock underweights long-dated IG). Sources: UBS House View, BlackRock Investment Institute, Morgan Stanley GIC, Citi Wealth, Goldman ISG, J.P. Morgan, Pictet.
